meta.title: 12.6 Checkpoint
meta.description: Design a comprehensive security architecture for a distributed system handling sensitive data.

blocks.content:
## Level 12 Checkpoint

Design the security layer for a distributed system.

blocks.question: A fintech startup is building a payment processing platform with 15 microservices on Kubernetes. It handles credit card numbers, bank account details, and transaction histories. Regulators require PCI-DSS compliance. Design the complete security architecture covering authentication, authorization, encryption, network security, and secrets management.
blocks.answer:
**Authentication:** OAuth 2.0 with OIDC for user-facing APIs. Auth0 or Keycloak as the IdP. JWTs (15-minute expiration) for stateless service-to-service auth. Refresh tokens stored in Redis with per-user revocation capability. Service-to-service: mTLS via Istio service mesh — each service has a cryptographic identity.

**Authorization:** RBAC for coarse roles (admin, merchant, support). ABAC layered on top for context-sensitive rules (merchants can only access their own transactions). OPA as the policy engine — authorization logic externalized from application code. Policies versioned in Git and deployed via CI/CD.

**Encryption:** Application-level encryption for PAN (card numbers) using Vault Transit engine — card numbers are never stored in plaintext, and the encryption key never leaves Vault. TDE on PostgreSQL for defense in depth. AES-256 with envelope encryption. TLS 1.3 for all client connections. mTLS for all internal service communication.

**Network security:** Dedicated VPC with public subnet (API gateway only) and private subnets (all services, databases). Kubernetes NetworkPolicies: default deny all, explicit allow per service pair. Only the tokenization service can reach the card vault database. WAF at the edge (AWS WAF) for OWASP top 10 protection. Istio AuthorizationPolicy for L7 service-to-service access control.

**Secrets management:** Vault with Kubernetes auth method. Dynamic database credentials with 1-hour TTL. Each service has a unique Vault policy granting access only to its required secrets. Vault audit logging enabled — every secret access is recorded for PCI-DSS compliance. Automatic TLS certificate rotation via cert-manager.

**PCI-DSS specific:** Cardholder data isolated in a dedicated Card Data Environment (CDE) subnet. Tokenization service sits at the CDE boundary — services outside the CDE only handle tokens, never raw card numbers. Quarterly vulnerability scans, annual penetration testing, comprehensive audit logging.

blocks.question: An e-commerce company discovers that an internal microservice has been compromised. The attacker has code execution on the order-service pod. Walk through what the attacker can and cannot do if the company has properly implemented the security patterns from this level.
blocks.answer:
**What the attacker CAN do:** Read the order-service's application memory (any secrets currently in memory). Make requests to services that the order-service is authorized to call (e.g., inventory-service, payment-service). Access the order database using the order-service's current dynamic credentials.

**What the attacker CANNOT do:** (1) Reach services the order-service does not talk to — Kubernetes NetworkPolicies block the traffic. (2) Impersonate another service — mTLS certificates are per-pod and managed by Istio, and the attacker cannot forge them. (3) Access other databases — dynamic credentials are scoped to the order database only (Vault least privilege). (4) Read secrets for other services — Vault policies restrict access. (5) Persist access beyond the credential TTL — dynamic credentials expire in 1 hour, and Vault can revoke all order-service leases immediately upon detection. (6) Exfiltrate data through unauthorized network paths — egress policies restrict outbound traffic.

**Detection and response:** Istio access logs show unusual request patterns from the order-service pod. Vault audit logs show credential access. The security team revokes all Vault leases for the order-service, rotates the order database credentials, and kills the compromised pod. Blast radius: limited to orders data for a maximum of 1 hour (dynamic credential TTL). Without these security layers, the attacker would have had access to every database, every secret, and every service in the cluster.

blocks.content:
## Ready for Level 13?

If you can design a layered security architecture covering identity, access control, encryption, network boundaries, and secrets management — and reason about blast radius when components are compromised — you are ready. Level 13 covers testing and reliability engineering.
